units.title: Basics 1
units.description: Learn your very first Portuguese words and sentences — people, food, and the verbs to be, to eat, and to drink.
units.guidebook:
Welcome to Portuguese! 🇧🇷🇵🇹

**Articles matter!** Every Portuguese noun has a gender. *O* = the (masculine), *a* = the (feminine). For example, *o homem* (the man) and *a mulher* (the woman).

**Ser vs. Estar** — both mean "to be." In this unit we use **ser** for identity: *Eu sou* (I am), *Você é* (You are).

**Verbs ending in -ar:** *comer* (to eat) → *eu como*, *você come*. *Beber* (to drink) → *eu bebo*, *você bebe*.

**Word order** is flexible, but Subject–Verb–Object is most common: *Eu como a maçã* (I eat the apple).

**Tip:** Brazilians often drop the subject pronoun because the verb form already tells you who's acting. So *Como pão* = "I eat bread."

Let's go! 🚀

units.title: Greetings
units.description: Learn essential Portuguese greetings and polite expressions for everyday conversations.
units.guidebook:
## Greetings in Portuguese 🇧🇷🇵🇹

Portuguese greetings change with the time of day and level of formality.

**Hello / Goodbye**
- **Olá** = Hello (universal, informal)
- **Bom dia** = Good morning (until ~noon)
- **Boa tarde** = Good afternoon (until sunset)
- **Boa noite** = Good evening / Good night

**How are you?**
- **Como vai?** = How are you? (formal)
- **Como estás?** = How are you? (informal, Portugal)
- **Tudo bem?** = Everything good? (very common)
- Reply: **Bem, obrigado/a** = Fine, thanks (m/f)

**Politeness**
- **Por favor** = Please
- **Obrigado** = Thank you (masc. speaker)
- **Obrigada** = Thank you (fem. speaker)
- **De nada** = You're welcome
- **Desculpe** = Excuse me / Sorry (formal)

**Goodbye**
- **Tchau** = Bye (informal)
- **Até logo** = See you later
- **Até amanhã** = See you tomorrow

💡 Tip: Brazilians often use **beijos** (kisses) and **abraços** (hugs) when greeting friends!

units.title: Food
units.description: Learn essential Portuguese words for common foods and drinks, and how to say you want, eat, or drink them.
units.guidebook:
Welcome to the Food unit! 🍽️

**Articles matter!** Portuguese nouns have grammatical gender. Masculine nouns typically use **o** (the) and feminine nouns use **a** (the). For example, *o pão* (the bread) is masculine, while *a água* (the water) is feminine.

**Wanting, eating, and drinking** use very common verbs:
- **Querer** (to want): *Eu quero café* (I want coffee)
- **Comer** (to eat): *Eu como arroz* (I eat rice)
- **Beber** (to drink): *Eu bebo sumo* (I drink juice)

**Word order** is flexible but typically follows Subject-Verb-Object. You can drop the subject pronoun (*eu*, *tu*) since the verb conjugation already tells you who's acting.

**A useful phrase:** *Eu quero um café, por favor* (I want a coffee, please) — perfect for any café in Lisbon! ☕

exercises.prompt: Eu como arroz todos os dias.
exercises.audioTarget: Eu como arroz todos os dias.

exercises.prompt: Eu como arroz todos os dias.
exercises.audioTarget: Eu como arroz todos os dias.

units.title: Animals
units.description: Learn the names of common animals in Portuguese and how to describe them simply.
units.guidebook:
Welcome to the world of animals in Portuguese! 🐾

**Genders matter!** Animal nouns have grammatical gender:
- **Masculine:** o gato (the male cat), o cavalo (the horse), o peixe (the fish)
- **Feminine:** a gata (the female cat), a vaca (the cow), a ave (the bird)

**Articles:** Use **o** for masculine and **a** for feminine singular nouns. Plural uses **os** and **as** (os cães, as vacas).

**Simple descriptions:** Use adjectives after the noun. They must agree in gender:
- o gato **pequeno** (the small cat)
- a vaca **grande** (the big cow)

**Useful verbs:**
- *ser* (to be) for permanent traits: *O cavalo é grande.*
- *ter* (to have) for possession: *Eu tenho um gato.*

**Fun fact:** *Cão* is the formal word for dog — *cachorro* is more common in everyday Brazilian Portuguese!

exercises.translation: The fish swims in the river.

units.title: Travel
units.description: Learn essential Portuguese vocabulary and phrases for getting around cities, taking trains, staying at hotels, and navigating airports and museums.
units.guidebook:
## Getting Around in Portuguese 🇧🇷🇵🇹

**Asking where things are** is one of the most useful travel skills. Use **Onde fica...?** (Where is...?) followed by a place. For example, *Onde fica o hotel?* (Where is the hotel?).

**Articles matter!** Portuguese nouns have grammatical gender. Masculine nouns use **o** (the) and feminine nouns use **a** (the). For example, *o aeroporto* (the airport) is masculine, while *a estação* (the station) is feminine.

**Prepositions of place** help with directions:
- **perto de** = near
- **longe de** = far from
- **em frente a** = in front of
- **ao lado de** = next to

**Verbs you'll hear often:**
- **ir** = to go (*Eu vou ao museu.* — I'm going to the museum.)
- **estar** = to be (location) (*O hotel está perto.* — The hotel is near.)
- **querer** = to want (*Quero um mapa.* — I want a map.)

**Polite phrases:** Always use **por favor** (please) and **obrigado/obrigada** (thank you, m/f). When asking for help, start with **Com licença** (Excuse me) to get attention politely.

**Pro tip:** Brazilians often use **a gente** instead of *nós* for "we" in speech — *A gente vai ao hotel.* (We're going to the hotel.)

exercises.prompt: Eu quero um mapa, por favor.
exercises.audioTarget: Eu quero um mapa, por favor.

exercises.audioTarget: Eu quero um mapa, por favor.

The bus stops on the street.
